past_medical_history: Chronic hypertension, diagnosed at age 27, managed with hydrochlorothiazide
Complicated migraine with aura, diagnosed at age 24

Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) six years ago during a prior pregnancy at 22 weeks gestation, complicated by bacterial endocarditis, left hip osteomyelitis, and a subacute left hemispheric stroke with full recovery
Gravida 2 para 0-1-0-0, currently at 18 weeks of gestation
ADAMTS13 activity measured one week prior to presentation was 16% of normal
family_history: Mother alive at age 60, history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Father alive at age 63, history of coronary artery disease with MI at age 55
Maternal grandmother deceased at age 72 from stroke
No known family history of hematologic disorders or thrombophilia

social_history.alcohol: None currently due to pregnancy; social drinker prior
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in single-family home
social_history.exercise: Light walking 3-4 times per week during pregnancy
social_history.illicit_drugs: Denies any current or past use
social_history.recent_events: Symptoms onset occurred during sexual intercourse

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Mildly anxious but cooperative. Speech is fluent with intermittent mild slurring noted by the examiner during sustained conversation. Follows complex commands appropriately. Attention and concentration grossly intact. No aphasia. MMSE not formally administered in the emergency setting.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal at 3 mm and reactive bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Mild left central facial droop with flattening of the left nasolabial fold. Mild left-sided facial weakness with lower face predominance, sparing the forehead. No ptosis. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. No diplopia. Right retro-orbital tenderness reported on palpation. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 throughout all four extremities at time of examination. No pronator drift. Normal muscle bulk and tone. Of note, the patient reports that approximately 90 minutes prior to current examination, she experienced approximately 30 minutes of left upper and lower extremity weakness that has since fully resolved.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports intermittent tingling in the left arm and leg that began approximately 60 minutes after initial symptom onset and persists at the time of examination. Light touch and pinprick sensation intact bilaterally. Proprioception intact. No sensory level.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ throughout bilateral upper and lower extremities. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing intact. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait not formally tested in the emergency department given pregnancy and recent acute symptoms; patient reports difficulty walking during the episode of weakness which has since resolved.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 2 (1 for mild facial palsy, 1 for mild dysarthria). The transient left hemiparesis has resolved by the time of neurological evaluation. Patient describes the right retro-orbital headache as moderate in severity, different in character from her typical migraines, with minimal photophobia.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset left-sided arm and leg weakness followed by tingling, slurred speech, and right-sided headache behind the eye
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Denise Watkins is a 35-year-old right-handed African American woman, gravida 2 para 0-1-0-0, currently at 18 weeks of gestation, who presents to the emergency department via ambulance with sudden onset neurological symptoms. The patient reports that she was at home this evening and experienced an abrupt onset of left-sided upper and lower extremity weakness during physical exertion. The weakness involved both her left arm and left leg and was significant enough that she was unable to grip objects with her left hand and had difficulty standing. The episode of weakness lasted approximately 30 minutes and then spontaneously resolved. Approximately sixty minutes after the initial onset of weakness, she developed new symptoms including tingling and numbness in her left arm and leg, slurred speech noticed by her husband, and a right-sided headache localized behind her right eye. She describes the headache as a dull, pressure-like pain with minimal sensitivity to light, which she reports is distinctly different in quality and location from her typical migraine headaches. Her husband called 911 when her speech became slurred. The patient has a significant medical history notable for an episode of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura six years ago during a prior pregnancy at 22 weeks of gestation, which was complicated by bacterial endocarditis, left hip osteomyelitis, and a subacute left hemispheric stroke from which she recovered fully. She is understandably anxious about a recurrence of TTP given the similarity of the current presentation to her prior event during pregnancy. She reports that her obstetrician checked an ADAMTS13 activity level approximately one week ago as part of routine monitoring given her TTP history, and it was reported as 16 percent of normal, which had been discussed with her hematologist. A transthoracic echocardiogram performed two weeks ago as part of her prenatal cardiology clearance was reportedly normal. She denies any fever, chills, joint pain, petechiae, dark urine, or bleeding. She denies any recent trauma, chiropractic manipulation, or vigorous neck movements. Her pregnancy has been otherwise uncomplicated to this point, with normal prenatal labs and anatomy scan pending.

findings.location: Right middle cerebral artery territory involving the right insular cortex and adjacent frontal operculum
findings.size: Approximately 2.2 x 1.5 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Hyperintense signal confirming restricted diffusion in the right MCA territory
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity in the right insular region, easily overlooked given early presentation
signal_characteristics.T2: Subtle hyperintensity in the right insular cortex and frontal operculum

findings.mass_effect: None. No midline shift. No evidence of hemorrhagic transformation.
findings.borders: Conforms to right MCA superior division territory distribution

findings.location: Right internal carotid artery at its origin (ostium)

signal_characteristics.MRA: Occlusion of the right middle cerebral artery just distal to the Circle of Willis. Irregular luminal contour of the right internal carotid artery at the ostium level.
signal_characteristics.T1_fat_sat: Crescent-shaped hyperintense intramural signal surrounding the right ICA at its ostium, consistent with intramural hematoma from arterial dissection

findings.location: Left cerebral hemisphere, left MCA territory
findings.size: Small area of encephalomalacia approximately 1.0 x 0.8 cm

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense gliotic change surrounding a small CSF-signal cavity
signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense focus consistent with old infarct

additional_observations: No hemorrhagic transformation of the acute infarct
Chronic left hemispheric infarct consistent with reported history of prior stroke six years ago
Magnetic resonance venography is negative for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
No additional acute areas of restricted diffusion beyond the right MCA territory
No evidence of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)
mri.impression: 1. Acute infarction in the right MCA superior division territory with restricted diffusion on DWI. 2. Occlusion of the right MCA just distal to the Circle of Willis. 3. Right internal carotid artery dissection at the ostium with irregular luminal contour and intramural hematoma on fat-saturated T1 sequences — likely the embolic source. 4. Chronic left hemispheric infarct consistent with known prior stroke history. 5. MR venography negative for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, right MCA territory secondary to right internal carotid artery dissection
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction in right MCA territory with ipsilateral ICA dissection at ostium on fat-sat T1 and MRA
Imaging pattern shows territorial infarct with clear vascular lesion (ICA dissection) rather than multifocal microvascular ischemia typically seen in TTP
ICA dissection provides a more proximal and parsimonious explanation; echocardiogram reportedly normal
Blood pressure not severely elevated, no PRES pattern on imaging, only 18 weeks gestation (pre-eclampsia typically later)

recommended_actions: Initiate antithrombotic therapy for carotid artery dissection — consider safety in pregnancy
Stat hematology consultation to evaluate for TTP relapse given history and low ADAMTS13
Repeat imaging in 24-48 hours to evaluate for infarct evolution or hemorrhagic transformation

findings: No atrial enlargement or ventricular hypertrophy
Physiologic changes consistent with pregnancy (slight leftward axis deviation)
ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead ECG. Normal sinus rhythm at 86 bpm with normal intervals and no acute abnormalities. Mild sinus tachycardia may reflect physiologic response to pregnancy and acute presentation.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No arrhythmia identified as a cardioembolic source. In a young pregnant patient with stroke, cardioembolism is less likely given normal ECG and reportedly normal echocardiogram. The differential should focus on arterial dissection, TTP-related thrombotic microangiopathy, and pregnancy-related hypercoagulability.

CBC.clinical_significance: Upper normal range, may be physiologic leukocytosis of pregnancy
Low-normal, consistent with physiologic anemia of pregnancy
Low-normal range. In the context of prior TTP, this value warrants close monitoring, though it does not meet criteria for thrombocytopenia.
Normal reticulocyte count argues against active hemolysis

CBC.value: No schistocytes identified; normocytic normochromic red blood cells
CBC.clinical_significance: Absence of schistocytes argues against active microangiopathic hemolytic anemia and active TTP

BMP.clinical_significance: Low-normal, may be related to hydrochlorothiazide use
Normal creatinine in pregnancy; no evidence of renal impairment from TTP
Mildly elevated, likely stress response; gestational diabetes should be screened at 24-28 weeks

Coagulation.clinical_significance: Normal to slightly elevated, consistent with pregnancy. Not suggestive of consumptive coagulopathy.
Mildly elevated but expected during pregnancy. Not diagnostic for DVT/PE in this context.

Hemolysis Workup.clinical_significance: Normal LDH argues against active hemolysis and TTP relapse
Normal haptoglobin argues against active intravascular hemolysis

Other.clinical_significance: Severely reduced ADAMTS13 activity is a known risk factor for TTP relapse and was being monitored by hematology. However, current CBC shows no evidence of active TTP (normal platelets, no schistocytes, normal hemolysis markers).

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are notable for severely reduced ADAMTS13 activity at 16% (from one week prior), low-normal platelet count of 154,000, mildly elevated glucose (likely stress response), and elevated D-dimer (expected in pregnancy). Critically, there is NO evidence of active TTP relapse: no schistocytes on peripheral smear, no thrombocytopenia, normal LDH, normal haptoglobin, and normal bilirubin. Coagulation studies are normal. The hemolysis workup is entirely negative. While the low ADAMTS13 places the patient at risk for TTP relapse, the current presentation is more consistent with an arterial thromboembolic event from a structural vascular cause rather than thrombotic microangiopathy.
abnormal_values_summary: ADAMTS13 activity 16% (severely reduced, from 1 week prior)
Glucose 108 mg/dL (mildly elevated, likely stress response)
D-dimer 1.2 mg/L FEU (elevated but expected in pregnancy)

findings.location: Right internal carotid artery at the ostium extending approximately 2 cm cranially

signal_characteristics.CTA: Irregular luminal narrowing of the right internal carotid artery beginning at its ostium with eccentric mural thickening. Residual lumen approximately 1.5 mm (normal 4.0 mm). No complete occlusion. Intimal flap visualized. String sign present.

findings.location: Right middle cerebral artery, just distal to the Circle of Willis

signal_characteristics.CTA: Complete occlusion of the right MCA M1 segment approximately 5 mm distal to its origin. No flow distal to the occlusion. Likely embolic occlusion from the ICA dissection.

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery, bilateral vertebral arteries, basilar artery, left MCA, bilateral ACA, bilateral PCA

signal_characteristics.CTA: Patent without stenosis, occlusion, or dissection. Normal caliber throughout.

additional_observations: No pseudoaneurysm formation at the dissection site
No evidence of dissection in any other cervical or intracranial vessel
Circle of Willis is complete with adequate cross-filling from left ICA via anterior communicating artery
Right ophthalmic artery patent with reconstitution from collateral flow
output.impression: 1. Right internal carotid artery dissection at the ostium with significant luminal narrowing (string sign), extending approximately 2 cm. 2. Embolic occlusion of the right MCA M1 segment, likely secondary to artery-to-artery embolism from the ICA dissection. 3. Remaining intracranial and cervical vasculature is patent and normal. 4. No fibromuscular dysplasia or other vasculopathy identified.
recommended_actions: Initiate antithrombotic therapy for carotid artery dissection — heparin anticoagulation preferred but must weigh risks in pregnancy
Follow-up vascular imaging in 3-6 months to assess for healing or progression
Multidisciplinary discussion with hematology, obstetrics, and neurology regarding management in pregnancy
Consider interventional options if neurological deterioration occurs

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: Intact, no PFO or ASD identified
Normal valvular structure and function. No vegetations. No regurgitation.
Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: No evidence of endocarditis, which is particularly relevant given history of prior bacterial endocarditis

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram reveals a structurally and functionally normal heart. LVEF is normal at 60%. No valvular vegetations identified, which is reassuring given the patient's history of prior bacterial endocarditis. No PFO, ASD, or intracardiac shunt identified. No intracardiac thrombus. This effectively excludes a cardioembolic source for the current stroke and confirms that the ICA dissection is the most likely mechanism.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Low-normal; may be physiologically decreased in pregnancy. Repeat testing post-partum recommended.
Reduced but expected during pregnancy. Protein S physiologically decreases to 30-50% during pregnancy. Must be repeated post-partum for accurate interpretation.

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel shows low Protein S activity at 45%, which is expected during pregnancy (physiologic decrease to 30-50%). All other thrombophilia markers are negative, including Factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, antiphospholipid antibodies, and lupus anticoagulant. Protein S should be repeated 6-8 weeks post-partum for definitive interpretation. The thrombophilia workup does not identify an additional hypercoagulable state beyond pregnancy itself and the known ADAMTS13 deficiency.
abnormal_values_summary: Protein S Activity 45% (reduced, expected in pregnancy)

findings: Normal sinus rhythm maintained throughout 48-hour recording
Average heart rate 82 bpm (range 62-128 bpm), mildly elevated average consistent with pregnancy
No episodes of atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other supraventricular tachycardia
Rare isolated premature atrial complexes (12 total over 48 hours)
output.interpretation: Forty-eight hour continuous cardiac monitoring demonstrates persistent normal sinus rhythm without any atrial arrhythmia. No paroxysmal atrial fibrillation or other arrhythmia identified.
output.clinical_correlation: Absence of arrhythmia on prolonged monitoring further supports a non-cardioembolic mechanism. Combined with the normal echocardiogram and the carotid artery dissection identified on MRI/CTA, the stroke mechanism is most consistent with artery-to-artery embolism from the ICA dissection.

signal_characteristics.Flow_pattern_right_ICA: Markedly elevated velocities with turbulent flow at the right ICA origin, consistent with high-grade stenosis from dissection

additional_observations: Left internal carotid artery demonstrates normal velocities and waveform
Bilateral common carotid arteries are normal without plaque or stenosis
Bilateral external carotid arteries are normal
Right ICA velocity elevation correlates with known dissection
output.impression: 1. Markedly elevated velocities in the right internal carotid artery (PSV 210 cm/s, EDV 95 cm/s, ICA/CCA ratio 3.8) consistent with high-grade stenosis from known dissection. 2. Normal left carotid system. 3. No atherosclerotic disease identified bilaterally, as expected in a 35-year-old.
recommended_actions: Serial duplex monitoring during antithrombotic therapy to assess dissection healing
Consider repeat imaging at 3 and 6 months

output.query: thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura stroke pregnancy ADAMTS13 deficiency management

results.title: TTP in Pregnancy: Diagnosis, Management, and Outcomes
results.authors: Scully M, Thomas M, Underwood M, et al.
results.key_finding: Pregnancy is a common trigger for TTP relapse, particularly in patients with congenital or acquired ADAMTS13 deficiency. Monitoring ADAMTS13 levels during pregnancy is recommended, with prophylactic plasma exchange if levels drop below 10%.

results.title: Cerebrovascular Events in TTP: Mechanisms and Differential Diagnosis
results.key_finding: Stroke in TTP typically presents with fluctuating neurological symptoms and is associated with thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. However, stroke-like symptoms with normal platelet count and no hemolysis markers should prompt evaluation for alternative etiologies.

results.title: Carotid Artery Dissection in Pregnancy: Systematic Review
results.authors: Kelly JC, Safain MG, Roguski M, et al.
results.journal: Journal of Maternal-Fetal and Neonatal Medicine
results.key_finding: Cervical artery dissection can occur during pregnancy and peripartum period. Hemodynamic and hormonal changes during pregnancy may increase arterial wall vulnerability. Anticoagulation with LMWH is considered safe in pregnancy.

output.summary: While ADAMTS13 deficiency below 10% is associated with TTP relapse risk during pregnancy, the diagnosis of active TTP requires evidence of thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Normal platelet count, absence of schistocytes, and normal hemolysis markers effectively exclude active TTP as the mechanism of stroke, even in patients with severely reduced ADAMTS13. Carotid artery dissection is a recognized cause of stroke in pregnancy and should be independently evaluated. Management of carotid dissection in pregnancy typically involves LMWH anticoagulation, which is safe for the fetus.

output.query: carotid artery dissection pregnancy anticoagulation LMWH management guidelines

results.title: Cervical Artery Dissection: Current Concepts and Management
results.key_finding: Antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation) is the mainstay of treatment. In pregnancy, LMWH is preferred over warfarin due to teratogenicity of vitamin K antagonists.

results.title: AHA/ASA Guidelines for Stroke Management in Pregnancy
results.authors: Bushnell C, McCullough LD, et al.
results.key_finding: LMWH and UFH are preferred anticoagulants during pregnancy. Warfarin is contraindicated in the first trimester and generally avoided throughout pregnancy. Aspirin at low doses (81 mg) is considered safe after the first trimester.

results.title: CADISS Trial: Antiplatelet vs Anticoagulation for Cervical Artery Dissection
results.authors: Markus HS, Levi C, King A, et al.
results.key_finding: No significant difference between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy in non-pregnant patients. Decision should be individualized in pregnancy.

output.summary: For carotid artery dissection during pregnancy, LMWH anticoagulation is the preferred treatment given the contraindication of warfarin during pregnancy. Low-dose aspirin (81 mg) can also be considered and is generally safe after the first trimester. The choice between anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy should be individualized with multidisciplinary input (neurology, hematology, obstetrics). Monitoring should include serial vascular imaging and neurological assessments. The concurrent ADAMTS13 deficiency does not change the acute dissection management but necessitates ongoing hematologic surveillance for TTP relapse.

output.proposed: Enoxaparin (LMWH) for carotid artery dissection in pregnancy
interactions: Hydrochlorothiazide: No significant interaction with enoxaparin.
Prenatal vitamins: No interaction. Ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D supplementation while on LMWH long-term.
contraindications: Active hemorrhage or conditions with high risk of uncontrolled bleeding
Severe thrombocytopenia (platelets <50,000) — not present in this patient
warnings: Monitor anti-Xa levels for therapeutic dosing in pregnancy, as volume of distribution changes
Consider switching to UFH at 36 weeks gestation for easier peripartum management
Neuraxial anesthesia requires LMWH discontinuation 24 hours prior to planned procedure
Concurrent ADAMTS13 deficiency: if TTP relapse occurs with developing thrombocytopenia, anticoagulation risk-benefit must be reassessed
LMWH does not cross the placenta and is considered safe for the fetus
alternatives: Unfractionated heparin (UFH) — alternative if renal clearance is concern or peripartum
Aspirin 81 mg daily — may be used alone or as adjunct, safe in pregnancy after first trimester
Fondaparinux — limited data in pregnancy, reserved for HIT

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, right middle cerebral artery territory, secondary to right internal carotid artery dissection at the ostium

differential.diagnosis: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura relapse with stroke
differential.likelihood: Moderate initially, excluded by lab findings
differential.key_distinguishing: The patient's history of TTP with severely reduced ADAMTS13 activity (16%) during pregnancy makes TTP relapse a compelling initial concern. However, normal platelet count (154,000), absence of schistocytes on peripheral smear, normal LDH, and normal haptoglobin effectively exclude active TTP. The territorial infarct pattern on MRI with identifiable vascular lesion further argues against the diffuse microvascular injury typical of TTP.

The patient has a history of complicated migraine with aura, and the transient hemiparesis followed by headache could suggest hemiplegic migraine. However, MRI shows acute infarction with restricted diffusion, and MRA reveals carotid dissection, confirming a structural vascular cause rather than a functional migrainous event.

Only 18 weeks of gestation (pre-eclampsia typically occurs after 20 weeks). Blood pressure is not severely elevated (132/78). No proteinuria, normal uric acid, and no PRES pattern on MRI. The focal territorial infarct pattern is inconsistent with the diffuse vasogenic edema of eclampsia.

Given history of prior bacterial endocarditis, this is worth considering. However, transthoracic echocardiogram shows no vegetations, normal valvular function, and no intracardiac thrombus. The ICA dissection on imaging provides a clear alternative embolic source.
Echocardiogram shows intact interatrial septum without PFO or ASD. No route for paradoxical embolism.

optimal_actions.action: Perform urgent neurological assessment and calculate NIHSS score to quantify deficits
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 2 with mild facial palsy and dysarthria; resolved hemiparesis by time of exam

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC with peripheral smear, BMP, coagulation studies, LDH, haptoglobin, and hemolysis workup to evaluate for TTP relapse
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of active TTP: normal platelets, no schistocytes, normal LDH and haptoglobin. This critical finding shifts the differential away from TTP relapse.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia as cardioembolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm, no arrhythmia — excludes cardioembolic mechanism

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI and MRA of head and neck including fat-saturated T1 sequences, plus MR venography
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute right MCA territory infarction; right ICA dissection at ostium with intramural hematoma; MRV negative for venous sinus thrombosis; chronic left hemispheric infarct from prior event

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck for detailed vascular characterization of the ICA dissection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right ICA ostial dissection with string sign and high-grade stenosis; embolic occlusion of right MCA M1

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antithrombotic therapy for carotid dissection — LMWH preferred in pregnancy (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg subcutaneously every 12 hours with anti-Xa monitoring)
Consult hematology for ADAMTS13 monitoring and TTP relapse surveillance despite currently negative hemolysis workup
Consult obstetrics for fetal monitoring and coordinated pregnancy management
Admit to stroke unit for serial neurological examinations and monitoring for infarct expansion or hemorrhagic transformation
Order echocardiogram to evaluate for cardiac source and rule out endocarditis recurrence
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal echocardiogram, no vegetations, no PFO — excludes cardioembolic source

optimal_actions.action: Order hypercoagulability panel to evaluate for additional prothrombotic conditions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Low Protein S (physiologic in pregnancy), otherwise negative. Repeat post-partum recommended.

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for anticoagulation safety in pregnancy with current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: LMWH is safe in pregnancy, no significant interactions with current medications

critical_actions: Exclude active TTP relapse by checking CBC with peripheral smear, LDH, and haptoglobin before attributing stroke to TTP
Obtain brain MRI with DWI and vascular imaging (MRA/CTA) including neck vessels to identify the ICA dissection
Initiate pregnancy-safe antithrombotic therapy (LMWH) for carotid artery dissection
Involve hematology for ongoing ADAMTS13 monitoring given severe deficiency and pregnancy, even though active TTP is excluded
Involve obstetrics for fetal monitoring and coordinated care
Monitor for infarct expansion and hemorrhagic transformation in acute stroke setting
contraindicated_actions: Initiating plasma exchange or caplacizumab for presumed TTP relapse without confirming active thrombotic microangiopathy (normal platelets and negative hemolysis workup exclude active TTP)
Administering warfarin during pregnancy due to teratogenic risk — LMWH is the appropriate anticoagulant
Administering IV thrombolysis (alteplase) in the setting of arterial dissection without careful risk-benefit analysis, particularly with concurrent pregnancy
Dismissing the presentation as complicated migraine without obtaining neuroimaging, given the history of prior TTP-related stroke
Delaying anticoagulation for the dissection while pursuing TTP-specific workup that is already negative
Performing catheter-based angiography as first-line evaluation without prior noninvasive imaging
key_reasoning_points: The history of TTP with severely reduced ADAMTS13 (16%) during pregnancy creates a strong anchoring bias toward TTP relapse as the stroke mechanism. The key to correct diagnosis is recognizing that active TTP requires thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, which are absent here.
Normal platelets (154,000), no schistocytes on peripheral smear, normal LDH, and normal haptoglobin effectively exclude active TTP despite the severely reduced ADAMTS13 activity.

The territorial infarct pattern (right MCA territory) with a clear proximal vascular lesion (ICA dissection) on MRA/CTA is diagnostic of artery-to-artery embolism from dissection, not the diffuse microvascular thrombosis of TTP.
Carotid artery dissection can occur during pregnancy, possibly related to hemodynamic and hormonal changes affecting the arterial wall.
Anticoagulation for carotid dissection in pregnancy requires LMWH rather than warfarin, with anti-Xa level monitoring due to altered pharmacokinetics in pregnancy.
The severely reduced ADAMTS13 still requires ongoing hematologic monitoring despite excluding active TTP, as the patient remains at risk for relapse throughout pregnancy.
The chronic left hemispheric infarct on MRI confirms the prior TTP-related stroke, which may further anchor clinicians toward a TTP diagnosis for the current event.

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle — young pregnant woman with history of TTP and severely reduced ADAMTS13 presenting with stroke symptoms creates strong anchoring bias toward TTP relapse. The key diagnostic challenge is recognizing that normal hemolysis markers and platelet count exclude active TTP, and that the territorial infarct pattern with carotid artery dissection on imaging points to a completely different mechanism. Multiple red herrings: prior TTP history, low ADAMTS13, pregnancy, prior endocarditis, and prior stroke.
clinical_pearls: Severely reduced ADAMTS13 activity is a risk factor for TTP but does not confirm active disease — active TTP requires thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia
Carotid artery dissection is an underrecognized cause of stroke in pregnancy and the peripartum period
Prior medical history creates powerful anchoring bias that can lead to incorrect diagnosis — always verify the mechanism with appropriate diagnostics
Territorial infarct patterns on MRI with DWI restriction are characteristic of large vessel thromboembolic disease, whereas TTP causes diffuse microvascular thrombosis
